raw_case:
Urethral clear cell adenocarcinoma in an adult female: A rare case report
Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra is an extremely rare malignancy with a poor outcome, mainly affecting females in old age. We present the case of a 42-year-old female patient who presented with progressively worsening lower urinary tract symptoms, leading to a cystoscopy-guided core needle biopsy diagnosis of clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra. We will mainly discuss the cross-sectional imaging and pathological aspects of the case.1

Introduction

Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra (CCAU) is a rare primary malignancy of the urethra, overwhelmingly affecting females.1 The mean age at disease onset is 58 years.2 Lower urinary tract symptoms, hematuria, and a recurrent urinary tract infection are present. CCAU is present in the bladder base and urethra. When it occurs in the urethra, around half of the cases arise from an underlying urethral diverticulum.3The histological origin of CCAU has been debated, but new research suggests that it has a urological origin. This is supported by genetic and immunohistochemical study results that are similar to those of urothelial carcinoma. These include changes in chromosomes 3, 7, and 17, as well as positivity for CD10 and CK7.4 Histologically, there are 3 main forms, namely the papillary, tubulocystic, and diffuse forms. We also see nuclear pleomorphism, cytoplasmic clearing, and cellular structural variations consisting of hobnailing, cuboidal, and flat structures.2,3,5Cystoscopy investigation enables direct tumor visualization and sample acquisition. Imaging modalities consist of pelvic ultrasound, computed tomography (CT), and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). They help to outline the full extent of the lesion in relation to adjacent organs.5, 6, 71.1

Case presentation

A 42-year-old female presented with hesitancy and poor urinary flow, as well as abnormally increased urinary frequency and urge incontinence for a 1-year duration. Before coming to our hospital, she had received multiple antibiotic treatments for a suspected urinary tract infection. Other than her urination problems, she reported no previous history of chronic illnesses. On presentation, the patient had stable vital signs, and the physical examination was unremarkable. The baseline blood workup includes the complete blood count, renal function tests, and serum electrolytes within the reference range. Urinalysis revealed a turbid appearance, blood +2, and many RBCs per HPF.The post-contrast abdominopelvic CT (Fig. 1) revealed a 3.7 cm × 3 cm x 2.7 cm (TRxCCxAP) enhancing mass of the entire urethra with superior extension to the bladder base. The MRI (Fig. 2, Fig. 3) revealed T1W intermediate, T2W bright, and enhancing masses that completely filled the urethral lumen and completely obliterated the normal urethral signal stratum.Both CT and MRI revealed no signs of abdominopelvic lymphadenopathy or solid organ secondaries. Upon cystoscopic evaluation, papillary growth filled the entire urethra, extending to the bladder neck. The bladder showed significant post-obstructive changes, including severe trabeculation and multiple diverticula. The bladder showed no growth, and both ureteric orifices were visible and appeared normal. We took multiple biopsy samples with transurethral resection and subjected them to pathological analysis. It was possible to see under a microscope that a lot of the tumor had mixed tubulopapillary and cribriform growth of highly pleomorphic cells, hobnailing, and cytoplasmic clear cell changes(Fig. 4). Immunohistochemical analysis (Fig. 5) showed strong and diffuse CK7 and PAX8 positivity. The tumor was negative for CK20, CDX2, PSA, and P63.A multidisciplinary team comprising urologists, oncologists, radiologists, and pathologists discussed the patient's case and offered her a radical cystourethrectomy with an ileal conduit after a thorough workup. We thoroughly counseled her on the procedure's details and potential complications. Unfortunately, the patient refused to consent to the procedure, citing her concern about the impact it would have on her quality of life given her low socioeconomic status. Despite repeated attempts to persuade her, the patient did not consent to the procedure until this report was written. She is currently voiding via a transurethrally placed catheter.2

Discussion

Urethral malignancies in general are extremely rare, accounting for less than 0.02 % of all malignancies in women.8 Since its initial description in 1973, a few case reports and case series have documented the even rarer CCAU, which represents only 10 % of these tumors.9 It is a distinct entity when compared to the commoner subtypes of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and transitional cell carcinoma (TCC) of the urethra. Previous case collections2,5 have noted a higher prevalence of urethral diverticulum. The largest case series is Olivia and Young,2 from 1996. According to their findings, 12/19 of CCAU occurred in the urethral diverticula.Currently clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra is listed under Mullerian-derived tumors in the 2022 WHO (world health organization) classification of tumors of the urinary system.10 Morphologically, the tubulocystic or papillary organization with hobnailing and clear or eosinophilic cytoplasm appeared similar to mullerian derived tumors of the female genital tract.10In addition, pathologic analysis in CCAU showed strong positivity for PAX8, a specific mesonephric or Mullerian tumor marker. GATA3 reactivity was negative, suggesting a non-urothelial origin 11. Genetic study of CCAU also revealed a significant lack of the TERT promoter mutation, the most prevalent urothelial carcinoma mutation.11Imaging is important for assessing the urethra, as a clinical examination can be difficult or non-revealing. MRI is the modality of choice for assessing the female urethra given its non-invasiveness, superior spatial and inherent tissue contrast, and high signal-to-noise ratio. The normal urethra measures 4 cm and spans from the bladder base to the external urethral meatus. Normally, the urethra on T2W and post-contrast T1W sequences has a layered or target-like appearance. From periphery to center, the signal intensity consists of a hypointense layer (muscular layer), a hyperintense layer (submucosa), a hypointense layer (epithelium of the mucosa), and a bright urine-filled lumen. Disruption of the normal appearance by a tumor signifies local invasiveness to adjacent structures such as the vagina and bladder.5, 6, 7Pretreatment planning uses MRI, which accurately assesses local tumor extension in 90 % of cases.6 MRI evaluates the size, location, and extent of tumors. These are critical because the prognosis heavily depends on the stage rather than the grade of the tumor.12 Tumors that involve the entire urethra (also called posterior tumors) drain to the external iliac, obturator, hypogastric, and paraaortic nodes and have a poorer outcome. Anterior tumors are limited to the distal one-third of the urethra and drain to inguinal nodes. They have a better outcome.7,12MRI cannot adequately forecast specific tumor histology and requires histologic examination.6,12 However, inherent urethral histology dictates tumor location, allowing for certain predictions to be made. SCC, which is the commonest type of urethral cancer, is usually located in the anterior or distal urethra. It is hypointense on T2W imaging. TCC has heterogeneous T1W and T2W signal intensities and commonly resides in the bladder neck. Adenocarcinomas tend to involve the entire urethral segment.12 On MRI, CCAU appears to be a heterogeneously enhanced exophytic mass with a high signal intensity on the T2W sequence. In a case as advanced as ours, with a lot of concentric growth and periurethral extension, it might be difficult to see the diverticular origin, and a cytopathologic confirmation is required.According to the European Association of Urology Guidelines on Primary Urethral Carcinoma13, our case represents T3N0M0 disease because it clearly invades the bladder neck and anterior vagina. We recommend a multimodal treatment approach in such cases, which combines definitive surgery with chemotherapy, either with or without radiotherapy. Such an approach is superior to surgery alone in prolonging survival. The prognosis for advanced cases is guarded by the overall 5-year survival for ≥ cT3 estimated at only 29 % versus ≤ cT1 disease, which carries a much more favorable rate of 63 %.14In conclusion, clear cell carcinoma of the female urethra is an extremely rare disease with a poor prognosis. Histologic and immunohistochemical analysis are important to make a definitive diagnosis. Early surgical treatment confers a better survival chance.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Yacob Sheiferawe Seman: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Michael Teklehaimanot Abera: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation. Fadil Nuredin Abrar: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tesfaye Kebede Legesse: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Mesfin Asefa Tola: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tsiyon Nigusie Alemu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization.

Informed patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for anonymized patient information to be published in this article.

Funding source

None.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 42-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressively worsening lower urinary tract symptoms.
- **History of Present Illness:** Hesitancy, poor urinary flow, increased urinary frequency, and urge incontinence for 1 year. Treated unsuccessfully for presumed urinary tract infections.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** Unremarkable; low socioeconomic status.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Stable vital signs; no significant findings.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Bloodwork: Normal (CBC, renal function tests, serum electrolytes).
  - Urinalysis: Turbid appearance; +2 blood; many RBCs/HPF.
  - Imaging: CT showed a 3.7 × 3 × 2.7 cm enhancing mass in the urethra extending to the bladder base but no lymphadenopathy or distant metastasis. MRI confirmed the mass with local invasion, filling the urethral lumen and obliterating the normal urethral signal structure.
  - Cystoscopy: Papillary growth occupying the urethra and extending to the bladder neck. Severe bladder trabeculation and diverticula noted, but no bladder growth.
  - Pathology: Biopsy revealed tubulopapillary and cribriform growth with pleomorphic cells, hobnailing, and clear cell cytoplasm. Immunohistochemical analysis was positive for CK7 and PAX8; negative for CK20, CDX2, PSA, and P63.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Differential Diagnoses:** Given the patient’s lower urinary tract symptoms and imaging findings of a urethral mass, possible conditions included:
  1. **Urethral diverticulum carcinoma** (e.g., clear cell or other adenocarcinomas): Supported by the mass's size, location, and imaging characteristics.
  2. **Squamous cell carcinoma:** Common urethral neoplasm but less likely given the imaging pattern and lack of squamous differentiation on histology.
  3. **Transitional cell carcinoma (TCC):** Often occurs at the bladder neck but ruled out due to immunohistochemical negativity for urothelial markers (GATA3).
  4. **Primary non-urothelial adenocarcinomas (e.g., colorectal origin):** Excluded due to negativity for CDX2 and CK20.
  5. **Metastatic disease:** No distant primary lesions identified on imaging.
  
- **Key Diagnostic Steps and Rationale:**
  - Imaging delineated a mass involving the entire urethra with bladder neck invasion.
  - Histopathology confirmed clear cell morphology with tubulopapillary structure.
  - Immunohistochemical positivity for CK7 and PAX8 aligned with clear cell adenocarcinoma of urethral/Müllerian origin.
  - Multidisciplinary consensus determined the diagnosis of urethral clear cell adenocarcinoma based on pathological and imaging findings.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra (T3N0M0 stage) with bladder neck invasion but no lymphadenopathy or distant metastasis.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 42-year-old female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressively worsening lower urinary tract symptoms.
- **History of Present Illness:** Hesitancy, poor urinary flow, increased urinary frequency, and urge incontinence for 1 year. Treated unsuccessfully for presumed urinary tract infections.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** Unremarkable; low socioeconomic status.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Stable vital signs; no significant findings.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Bloodwork: Normal (CBC, renal function tests, serum electrolytes).
  - Urinalysis: Turbid appearance; +2 blood; many RBCs/HPF.
  - Imaging: CT showed a 3.7 × 3 × 2.7 cm enhancing mass in the urethra extending to the bladder base but no lymphadenopathy or distant metastasis. MRI confirmed the mass with local invasion, filling the urethral lumen and obliterating the normal urethral signal structure.
  - Cystoscopy: Papillary growth occupying the urethra and extending to the bladder neck. Severe bladder trabeculation and diverticula noted, but no bladder growth.
  - Pathology: Biopsy revealed tubulopapillary and cribriform growth with pleomorphic cells, hobnailing, and clear cell cytoplasm. Immunohistochemical analysis was positive for CK7 and PAX8; negative for CK20, CDX2, PSA, and P63.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
### Reasoning:
The patient presents with a constellation of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) that have been progressively worsening over a year, including hesitancy, poor urinary flow, increased frequency, and urge incontinence. Hematuria is also noted on urinalysis. These symptoms are highly suggestive of a significant obstructive lesion within the lower urinary tract, specifically the urethra or bladder neck.

Ancillary investigations revealed a substantial mass (3.7 × 3 × 2.7 cm) located in the urethra, extending to the bladder base, confirmed by CT and MRI. The MRI specifically notes that it fills the urethral lumen and obliterates the normal urethral structure, explaining the obstructive symptoms. Cystoscopy further characterized this as a papillary growth occupying the urethra and extending to the bladder neck, with secondary bladder changes (trabeculation, diverticula) indicative of chronic obstruction.

The pathology report is crucial for definitive diagnosis. The biopsy describes a tubulopapillary and cribriform growth pattern with pleomorphic cells exhibiting hobnailing and clear cell cytoplasm. This combination of architectural patterns and specific cellular morphology is highly suggestive of a particular type of adenocarcinoma.

Immunohistochemical analysis provides critical discriminative information. The positive staining for CK7 and PAX8, coupled with negative staining for CK20, CDX2, PSA, and P63, helps narrow down the differential diagnosis significantly. CK7 is commonly found in adenocarcinomas of the urothelial tract and Müllerian epithelium. PAX8 is characteristically positive in renal cell carcinoma and Müllerian epithelium, and can be positive in some urothelial carcinomas but is less typical. The negativity for CK20 and CDX2 argues against a colonic or intestinal origin. P63 negativity argues against a pure urothelial or squamous cell carcinoma.

Considering the location (urethra), histological features (clear cell change, hobnailing, tubulopapillary/cribriform pattern), and the IHC profile (CK7+, PAX8+, CK20-, P63-), the primary diagnosis is Clear Cell Adenocarcinoma of the Urethra. This entity can arise *de novo* in the urethra, potentially from urethral glands which may exhibit Müllerian-type differentiation, hence explaining the PAX8 positivity. The clear cell morphology is reminiscent of clear cell renal cell carcinoma, but the primary site identified is the urethra. The absence of distant metastasis on CT is noted, but the local invasion is evident.

### Answer:
Clear Cell Adenocarcinoma of the Urethra

judgment_path.info:
Tubulopapillary carcinoma (TPC) of the breast is an aggressive subtype characterized by infiltrating tubules and papillary projections, with a higher prevalence of triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). Symptoms may include palpable masses or changes observed in imaging studies. Diagnosis involves histopathologic assessment, with at least 50% of the tumor exhibiting tubulopapillary features. Immunohistochemical markers p16, p53, and SOX10 are significant for classification, with a notable presence of abnormal p53 staining and diffuse SOX10 expression in TN tumors. Clinical findings indicate a median patient age of 66 years, with tumors typically ranging from 0.5 to 7.8 cm. Treatment approaches included surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation, and follow-up demonstrated no evidence of disease in 91% of patients over a median period of 13 months. Further molecular profiling through next-generation sequencing is pending to enhance understanding of TPC.

Papillary neoplasms of the breast include various tumor types ranging from benign intraductal papillomas to in situ and invasive papillary carcinomas. Symptoms may include non-bloody or bloody nipple discharge and palpable masses, particularly associated with large intraductal papillomas. Diagnosis typically involves imaging and histopathological assessment, specifically looking for architectural features and the presence or absence of myoepithelial cells, which aid in distinguishing between benign and malignant entities. Key types include intraductal papilloma, characterized by finger-like projections and potential coexistence with atypical hyperplasia; papillary ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), identified by the absence of myoepithelial cells and the presence of invasive characteristics; encapsulated papillary carcinoma, which presents in a cystic space without myoepithelial cells; and solid-papillary carcinoma, recognized for its solid growth pattern. Risk assessment must consider the presence of atypical hyperplasia or DCIS, as multiple papillomas are associated with a higher breast cancer risk compared to solitary papillomas. Molecular studies suggest genetic alterations may contribute to the pathology of these tumors and their progression from benign to malignant forms.

reasoning_eval.step: Immunohistochemical analysis provides critical discriminative information. The positive staining for CK7 and PAX8, coupled with negative staining for CK20, CDX2, PSA, and P63, helps narrow down the differential diagnosis significantly. CK7 is commonly found in adenocarcinomas of the urothelial tract and Müllerian epithelium. PAX8 is characteristically positive in renal cell carcinoma and Müllerian epithelium, and can be positive in some urothelial carcinomas but is less typical. The negativity for CK20 and CDX2 argues against a colonic or intestinal origin. P63 negativity argues against a pure urothelial or squamous cell carcinoma.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Immunohistochemistry, CK7, PAX8, CK20, CDX2, PSA, P63, adenocarcinoma, Müllerian epithelium, renal cell carcinoma, urothelial tract, squamous cell carcinoma
judgment_path.info:
The study investigates the utility of cytokeratin-7 (CK7) and cytokeratin-20 (CK20) immunohistochemical stains in predicting the site of origin of tumors, based on retrospective analysis of 612 surgical pathology cases. Symptoms associated with the diseases being diagnosed were not explicitly noted; however, inconsistent CK7 and CK20 staining patterns were correlated with diagnostic challenges. Results showed that CK7 and CK20 stains contributed majorly to diagnosis in only 5% of dual-stained cases, 34% of CK7-only, and 69% of CK20-only cases. Notably, many cases presented staining patterns that contradicted final diagnoses or provided no specific diagnosis at all. The study emphasizes the need for judicious use of these stains, given their limited diagnostic utility and the potential for unnecessary healthcare costs.



Immunohistochemistry (IHC) has become a crucial diagnostic tool for metastatic carcinomas of unknown primary site (CUPs). The typical symptoms include the presence of metastatic tumors without identifiable primary sites, which can be challenging to classify despite advanced investigations. IHC aids in distinguishing these carcinomas into subtypes such as adenocarcinomas, poorly differentiated carcinomas, and squamous cell carcinomas by using organ-specific immunomarkers alongside staining patterns like CK7 and CK20. A systematic approach can classify most undifferentiated tumors accurately. Diagnosis primarily relies on clinical history, patient demographics, and IHC findings. Some of the common origins of CUPs identified through IHC include breast, colorectal, ovarian, and lung cancers. However, challenges such as small sample sizes and intra-tumoral heterogeneity can complicate the diagnostic process. In cases where IHC does not yield clear results, molecular profiling may provide additional insights into tumor characteristics and potential treatment responses. Overall, IHC contributes significantly to the diagnostic accuracy and management of CUPs.

reasoning_eval.step: Considering the location (urethra), histological features (clear cell change, hobnailing, tubulopapillary/cribriform pattern), and the IHC profile (CK7+, PAX8+, CK20-, P63-), the primary diagnosis is Clear Cell Adenocarcinoma of the Urethra. This entity can arise *de novo* in the urethra, potentially from urethral glands which may exhibit Müllerian-type differentiation, hence explaining the PAX8 positivity. The clear cell morphology is reminiscent of clear cell renal cell carcinoma, but the primary site identified is the urethra. The absence of distant metastasis on CT is noted, but the local invasion is evident.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Urethral clear cell adenocarcinoma,Histological features of clear cell adenocarcinoma,Immunohistochemical profile CK7+,PAX8 positivity in tumors,Müllerian-type differentiation,Clear cell renal cell carcinoma,Urethral gland pathology,Differential diagnosis for clear cell morphology,Distant metastasis on CT,Local invasion of cancer
judgment_path.info:
Primary urethral clear-cell adenocarcinoma is a rare and aggressive tumor predominantly affecting women, associated with a poor prognosis. Symptoms often include gross hematuria and abdominal pain. Diagnosis typically involves pathological evaluation revealing a distinctive histomorphology characterized by clear cytoplasm and nuclear atypia, often accompanied by positive immunohistochemistry markers like PAX8 and CK7. Next-generation sequencing of tumor samples has identified significant molecular alterations, including somatic mutations in ATM and ALK, focal copy number losses at the SMAD4 and ARID2 loci, and several gene fusions. Patients commonly present at advanced stages, and prognosis is influenced by the tumor's location; distal urethral tumors generally have a better outcome than proximal ones. Comprehensive diagnostic analysis, including urine cytological evaluation, may aid in confirming the diagnosis in cases where surgical specimens are limited.

Primary clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra (PCCAU) is an exceptionally rare malignant tumor characterized by nonspecific symptoms, commonly including voiding dysfunction, gross hematuria, dysuria, and the presence of a urethral mass. Diagnosis is primarily based on imaging modalities such as ultrasound (US), computed tomography (CT), and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which reveal irregular masses with heterogeneous enhancement and necrotic changes. In one reported case, a 58-year-old woman presented with voiding abnormalities and hematuria, with imaging demonstrating a hypoechoic mass, confirmed postoperatively as PCCAU through histopathological examination. Immunohistochemical markers supported the diagnosis. The case highlights the critical role of imaging and pathological correlation in diagnosing this rare malignancy, which can demonstrate aggressive behavior with a tendency for metastasis, underscoring the importance of early intervention and personalized treatment approaches.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Initial differential diagnoses considered included urethral diverticulum carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, transitional cell carcinoma (TCC), primary non-urothelial adenocarcinomas, and metastatic disease.
<Step 2> Urethral diverticulum carcinoma was supported by the mass's size, location, and imaging characteristics.
<Step 3> Squamous cell carcinoma was deemed less likely due to the imaging pattern and lack of squamous differentiation on histology.
<Step 4> Transitional cell carcinoma was ruled out based on immunohistochemical negativity for urothelial markers such as GATA3.
<Step 5> Primary non-urothelial adenocarcinomas were excluded due to immunohistochemical negativity for CDX2 and CK20.
<Step 6> Metastatic disease was excluded as no distant primary lesions were identified on imaging.
<Step 7> Imaging revealed a mass involving the entire urethra with bladder neck invasion.

<Step 8> Histopathology showed clear cell morphology with tubulopapillary structure, and immunohistochemical positivity for CK7 and PAX8 supported clear cell adenocarcinoma of urethral/Müllerian origin.
<Step 9> Multidisciplinary consensus established the diagnosis as urethral clear cell adenocarcinoma based on pathological and imaging findings.
<Step 10> Final diagnosis: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra (T3N0M0 stage) with bladder neck invasion but no lymphadenopathy or distant metastasis.
